name: Wingy Manone
abstract: Joseph Matthews "Wingy" Manone (February 13, 1900 – July 9, 1982) was an American jazz trumpeter, composer, singer, and bandleader. His recordings included "Tar Paper Stomp", "Nickel in the Slot", "Downright Disgusted Blues", "There'll Come a Time (Wait and See)", and "Tailgate Ramble".

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: Salute to Eddie Condon

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Loyacano
about_quotes.evidence: I worked in Texas with Wingy Manone. San Antonio, Texas. ... Yeah, that was Wingy's band, yeah.

about_quotes.speaker: Bud Freeman
about_quotes.evidence: Wingy... took us out of high school... we worked on Wednesday nights... at a ballroom called the Merry Garden Ballroom... Wingy Manone was playing in a place called The Brass Rail... I fronted the band.

about_quotes.speaker: Zutty Singleton
about_quotes.evidence: Yes, I was with Wingy Manone (Wingy?) (Lemone?) out in California.

about_quotes.speaker: Art Hodes
about_quotes.evidence: we had Paul Mares, we had Martie Marcella, we had this trumpet player from New Orleans that had spent years in Vegas, Louis Prima, and Wingy Manone. Four trumpets at the same time on stage

about_quotes.speaker: Artie Shaw
about_quotes.evidence: The one with Wingy Manone? ... Wingy went up to Harlem the night before, sat down with some arranger, and sang the themes to him. Then we got to the date.
about_quotes.doc_title: Artie Shaw — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Charlie Barnet
about_quotes.evidence: And we had a rousing finale with both bands on stage and everybody, a whole bunch of musicians, Wingy Manone jumped up there
